Fern Polrrrcer. PRACTTcEs Colrrr,rrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
September 29,2006
Charles H. Bell, Jr.
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk,LLp
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, CA 95814
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-06-138
Dear Mr. Bell:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").I Because your question is general in
nature, we provide you with informal written assistance.2
when is a sponsor's payrnen, "il:il:::ing expenses orits sponsored pAC
treated as a "contribution... for the purpose of making contributions to candidates for
elective state office," subject as such to the contribution limits of section g5303(a)?
CONCLUSION
The answer to this question begins and ends withthe purpose of the payment.
Contributions "for the purpose of making contributions to candidates for eleltive state
office" include payments used to generate such contributions. The ultimate purpose may
be inferred from the way the money is used. But notwithstanding yorr, ,rgglstion, ways
and means that"facilitate" accomplishment of a purpose may be evidenc"-of th" purpose
no less valid than methods more "directly', aimed at the same end result.
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 1g 109-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not offer the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal written
advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).)
iqrq,,4g


File No. I-06-138
Page No. 2
FACTS
You represent a number of sponsored state political committees (,?ACs,) and
their sponsors. For some of these, you act as treasurer. Many organizations that sponsor
these PACs pay all orpart of their PACs' fundraising expenses. you tell us that,.[t1h"
raison d'etre of a sponsored PAC is to raise funds to male contributions.,, In an earlier
request for advice, which resulted in our Advice Letter No. I-06-0 7l , youinquired into
the various kinds ofpayments by a sponsor to its sponsored committee that might be
treated as a "contribution... made for the purpose of making contributions to candidates
for elective state office," subject as such to the contributiorli-it, of section S5303(a).
You now refine the original inquiry. As we understand your premises, you
believe that a sponsor's contribution to its PAC, actually solicited and used for making
monetary or non-monetary contibutions to a candidate for elective state office, would be
a contribution governed by the limits of section 85303(a). You observe that the same
result obtains when the sponsor pays for a PAC fundraiser conducted at the behest of a
candidate for elective state office, to raise and transmit contributions to that candidate.
In both of these cases, the sponsor makes a contribution to its pAC in order to
generate further contributions to the candidate. As you phrase it, the sponsor in these two
examples makes a contribution that "is then used or is ai least useable to make another
monetary on non-monetary contribution at the behest of a candidate for elective state
office." Such payments are limited by section 85303(a) because they are contributions
"made for the purpo_se of making contributions." These contributions are recouped from
proceeds of the fundraising event and are then transmitted to the candidate, along with
any additional sums generated by the event.
As we understand your position, you believe that the foregoing examples may
illustrate the only circumstances where a sponsor's payment for a paC fundraiser would
be governed by section 85303(a). In those cases, yo,, .uy that there is a direct connection
between the contributions flowing into the PAC and the 
-funds 
flowing out from the pAC
to the candidate, after the fundraiser has been held.
In contrast, you note that when a sponsor makes payments to a committee for the
pu{pose of supporting regular, ongoing committee "*p".r."s, those payments cannot be
treated as "contributions" because a sponsor's payment towards the administration of a
committee is excluded from classification as a "contribution" by regulation 1g215(cX16).
You observethat a sponsor's payment of committee staff salary unJd"bt, owed to third-
party vendors are expenses of committee "administration," and contend that this remains
true even when those services were rendered in support a committee fundraiser.
You consider these latter payments to be "indirect" administrative support of a
fundraising event, distinguishable from the "direct" contributions illustratea in ttre first
two examples because:


File No. I-06-138
Page No. 3
^rl]f." 
sponsor's paymentof its pAC's administrative expenses merery
facilitated the making of contributions to candidates for elective state
office, that 'contribution in'... would not be part of the .orr." of any
'contribution out'.,,
Your question, in effect, is whether a payment that ..merel y facilitated,, the
making of contributions to candidates ror etective rtui"om"" ir lr;;ltiliin'guishabte
from a "contributiol, '1nade for the pu{p. ose of making contributions to candidates for
elective state office.".,The practicarrigrin"ur"" 
"itrrir-q"estion is very great. section
85303(a) limits contributions to a.orrr]ritt". (other thanfpoht icalpartycommittee) to
$5'000 per calendar year - when the contribution is made for the purpose of making
contributions to candidates for elective state office. All other payments to such
committees, incruding contributions, are not rimited. (section g5303(c).)
Regulation 18215 clarifies the Act's definition of the term .,contribution,,, which
isgivenatsection 82015. subdivision(c)ofregulation lg2l5containsalistof sixteen
kinds of payments that are deemed not to be "cintributions,, within the meaning of the
Act. In particular, subdivision (c)(16) excludes:
"A plymentby asponsoring organization for the
establishment and administration of a sponsored
committee, provided such payments are reported. Any
monetarypayment made under this subdivision to the
sponsored committee shalr be made by separate instrument.
A .sponsoring organization, may be u.ry p".ron (see Gov,t
code s^92947) except a candiditeor otire. individual (see
Gov't code g s2049.7). 'Estabrishment and adminis_
tration, means the cost of office space, phones, salaries,
utilities, supplies, legal and accounting f""r, ura other
expenses incurred in setting up and *rrrirg-u sponsored
committee.,,
ANALYSIS
The intent of this regulation, as we explained in our prior letter, is to exclude from
classification as "contribution" payments made for establishment and administration of a
sponsored committee.', In adopting this rule, the Commission openlychose not to extend
its provisions to payments made to underwrite a committee's fundraising costs. In fact,
you proposed a draft amendment specificall y including an exemption for payment of
committee fundraising costs, which the Commission twice declined to insert.3 There is
no basis for concluding that subdivision (c)(16) was ever intended (or can now be read)
to exempt payment of committee fundraising costs from the definition of "contribution.,,
,l$;**cted 
in the Minutes of the commission's special meetings on December 30, lgg6rmd January


File No. I-06-138
Page No. 4
You confront this problem by offering a distinction between ,,direct,payment of
fundraising costs, as against "indirect" paymJnts that "merel y facilitated,, the conduct of
fundraisers. As we understand the proposed distinction, you would agree that asponsor
would be subject to the contribution ti*it of section ss:ri:1a; if it paii u tt ira partyto put
together a fundraiser on behalf of the sponsored committee, to enable the committee to
contribute the proceeds to candidates for elective state office. But irthe sfonsor paid
committee staff and their vendors to accomplish the very same pulpose, those payments
would "merely facilitate" the fundraising event and would exemptihe ,po*o, from the
contribution limits of section S5303(a).
We believe that apayment to "facllitate" the making of a contribution is also a
payment made for the "purpose" of making the contribution. Moreover, we believe that
all of the payments you have described in your examples are payments to .,facili tate,, the
making of contributions, and that there is no principled basis roi "ut"goiring payments to
athird-party fundraiser as o'contributions,- *iril" payments to a cornmittee employee
performing the same task would be classified as something quite different.
We cannot find in the text of regulation 18215(c)(16), especially in tight of its
legislative history, any indication that a sponsor may treatpayments that*facilitate,, the
conduct of fundraising_events as payments for "the establishment and administration of a
sponsored committee." It would be particularly difficult to read regulation 1g215(c) in
such a fashion, since exceptions to astatute or regulation are read riarrowly, at least in the
absence of circumstances compelling departure iom this settled rule. As ive recently
noted in the McCowan Advice Letter,No. A_05_179:
"It is fundamental cannon of statutory construction that exceptions are to be
construed st{ctfy and narrowry." (Ascarate Advice Letter, No. A-04_0r2;
citing Deits ch Advice Letter, No. A-02- I 29 quoting Tickei Track Ca lifurnia,
Inc. v. Department of Motor vehicles eoo2) 97 ca1.App.4th 125r; ttg ca.
Rptr.2d 176.)
No less pertinent in the present context:
"That construction is favored which would defeat subterfuges,
expediencies,_or evasions employed to continue the mischiif sought to be
remedied by the statute, or to defeat compriance with its terms, ;;;;
attempt to accomplish by indirection what the statute forbids.,, (shaw v.
McMahan (1987) 197 Cal.App.3d 417,430.)
Ultimately, our concern with your proposal is that it is crafted precisely to change
the legal character ofpayments that cover the cost of a fundraising "r*t 
*inJirectly,,,by
hiring a staff member for example, to perform the services of a fuidraising.ortru"to..
The Act's definition of "contribution" at section 82015 is broad by design, sweeping in


File No. I-06-138
Page No. 5
from the outset any "payment. '. unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that
it is not made for political purposes.,,
Payments made to a PAC are especially likely to have been made for a political
purpose' Indeed, you noted in your original request ior advice that,,the *iron d,etre of a
sponsored PAC is to raise funds to make contributions." we cannot concurin aproposed
construction of regulation 18215(c)(16) that ignores a core function of the pACs you
represent, by reading into the regulation u -"Jh*irm that would convert fundraising
costs into "costs of establishment and administration," effectively resurrecting the 1995
proposal that was rejected by the Commission.
we advise instead that the answer to your question is found in the language of
section 85303 itself, which imposes a contributionlimit in subdivision (a) on payments
made "for the pu{pose of making contributions to candidates for electivL state office,,,
while subdivision (c) expressly lifts those limits for payments made for other putposes.
rtisthepurpose of thepayment, notmechanics or.iitiig,thattriggers the contribution
limits of section 85303(a). Thus when a sponsored comriitte" i. oi"*itt be worting on a
fundraising event, using committee resources to do so, you should conclude that most
sponsor payments for this use of committee resources (or payment of vendor invoices)
are made for the pu{pose of raising funds, not for the routine administration of the pAc.
To illustrate, payments made by a sponsor and used by the committee to pay
fundraising consultants (or committee staffers q".f"*;; equivalent services), for the
production and dissemination of fundraising solicitations"ana similar literature, for room
rental' food and beverage, entertainment, al,ong with similar goods and services, will all
be subject to the limits of section 85303(a) when the committee makes contributions to
candidates for state elective office. of course,-p"y-""dy the sponso, to support trre
committee's processing and reporting of contribuiions and expenditures, performed by
the committee treasurer in the ,o.rrut course of his or her duties, would properly be
classified as costs of administration. But except in unusual circumstances, we would
expect that such administrative expenses would amount only to a small fraction of a
committee's overall fundraising costs.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
/)L,*-/ a,LJLJ
Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
